description: Literature coverage/contact-area database for k_spread calibration of plastic_coverage.py against DEM outputs. Each entry captures exact process conditions needed to decide comparability with our single-compression pellet setup.

our_baseline.metric_type: 3D surface area coverage (DEM contact-area / AM surface)
our_baseline.note: DEM value is BEFORE k_spread calibration to physical coverage

entries.citation: Lee et al., Nature Communications 15, 2024 — Shear force effect of the dry process on cathode contact coverage in all-solid-state batteries

coverage_metric.definition: coverage = (SE-touching AM perimeter) / (total AM perimeter); yellow=AM edge, red=void edge, remainder=SE contact

si_highlights.why_useful: Direct comparison base for our contact-model constants
Confirms coverage = perimeter ratio (2D), not 3D surface area; lower bound vs our 3D DEM value
si_highlights.impact_on_our_k_spread: Cannot directly equate 30.6% (2D perimeter) to our 22% (3D surface). Need perimeter-to-surface geometric factor (~1.3-1.5x for spherical AM).

assembly_order: cathode pressed at 7 TON on SE

si_highlights.why_useful: Performance vs coverage trend establishes coverage as dominant variable

extracted.note: Dry > Wet > Hand for rate capability, correlating with coverage

si_highlights.why_useful: Their DEM also uses Hooke/Hysteresis or Hertz — can reuse as benchmark

comparability_with_ours.pressure_match: PARTIAL (their 520 MPa vs our 300 MPa — ~73% pressure, expect slightly lower coverage)
comparability_with_ours.chemistry_match: CLOSE (NCM532 vs our NCM, LPSCl vs our LPSCl)

k_spread_implication.caveat: 2D-to-3D correction needed first; true target likely 0.35-0.45 in 3D terms → k_spread ≈ 1.26-1.43

entries.citation: Bielefeld, Weber, Janek. J. Phys. Chem. C 2019, 123, 1626-1634 — Microstructural Modeling of Composite Cathodes for All-Solid-State Batteries
entries.caveat_upfront: Does NOT report coverage (%) directly. Reports specific active interface area A_spec,a (m²/m³) and utilization level. Also does NOT give τ(composition) or σ_eff(composition) curves despite earlier expectation. Still Tier 1 because it validates percolation exponent and optimal composition for our scaling law.

process.pressure_match_with_ours: NO (no physical pressure, just geometric packing)
process.overlap_treatment: AM spheres = NO overlap (hard-sphere packing, <1e-5 vol% residual); SE polyhedra = WITH overlap; merge step assigns overlap to AM (SE loses volume), AM stays spherical

power_law_fit_from_fig4.usefulness: Direct equation — we can plug (p - pc) from our dataset's porosity/composition to validate exponent 0.41 against our √(φ-0.2) term

key_results.beta_comparison: Matches Sur et al. 1976 3D site-percolation simple-cubic lattice — VALIDATES our scaling law using percolation-like exponents (CN^1.5, √(φ-0.2), etc.)

electrode_thickness_effect.effect_on_thin_electrode_percolation: thin electrodes appear to percolate at lower AM vol% — FINITE SIZE ARTIFACT, not real advantage

coverage_equivalent_derivation.reported_metric: A_spec (m²/m³) — volumetric surface area of ELECTRONIC cluster (AM-AM connectivity, not AM-SE interface)
coverage_equivalent_derivation.caveat: Fig 3-5 show electronic cluster only. AM-SE interface (A_spec,a) is in Fig 7-8 — user has NOT provided those figures yet.
coverage_equivalent_derivation.how_to_convert_to_coverage_frac: electronic-cluster coverage ≈ A_spec / A_spec,geo, where A_spec,geo = g_AM^V · 6/d_AM (eq 7)

digitized_from_fig3_5um_porosity_20pct.comment: 5μm AM, 20% porosity, varying AM vol%. Values read ±10% from plot.

digitized_from_fig5_saturation_by_size.comment: A_spec saturation at AM=65 vol%, 20% porosity — shows 1/d scaling (confirms 6/d geom limit).

coverage_equivalent_derivation.key_insight: At saturation (far above pc), electronic cluster covers ~92-96% of AM regardless of particle size. Particle-size dependence is in WHERE the transition happens (pc), not in HOW MUCH saturates.

AM_SE_interface_coverage_from_fig7_8.purpose: THIS IS THE QUANTITY FOR k_spread CALIBRATION — AM-SE active interface area A_spec,a
AM_SE_interface_coverage_from_fig7_8.convention: coverage = A_spec,a / A_spec,geo, where A_spec,geo = g_AM^V · 6/d_AM

fig7_5um_20pct_porosity_vs_composition.comment: Peak A_spec,a ≈ 3.3e5 m²/m³ around AM/SE = 70/30 vol%. Electronic limit below 69/31, ionic limit above 79/21.

fig8_porosity_comparison_5um.comment: Same sweep at 3 porosities. Denser electrodes → much higher interface area.
fig8_porosity_comparison_5um.closest_packing_limit_line: Diagonal cutoff beyond ~85/15 vol% — near 74% hard-sphere packing fraction

fig9_porosity_sweep_at_70_30_vol_5um.comment: Constant composition 70/30 AM/SE vol%, 5μm, porosity swept 43% → 3%. Continuous version of Fig 8's 3 discrete points. Shows three regimes: (i) ionic+electronic limitation above ~34% porosity; (ii) electronic limitation between 21-34%; (iii) well-connected below 21%.

cross_check_with_fig8.agreement: Fig 9 composition is 70/30 (fixed), Fig 8 peak compositions are at 65/35 (5%, 10%) or 70/30 (20%) — small offset explains 5-7% discrepancy at low porosity. At 20% both match exactly.

fit_attempt.functional_form: coverage(phi) ≈ 0.78 * tanh(k * (phi_c - phi))  for phi < phi_c ≈ 0.35
fit_attempt.usefulness: Smooth monotonic function of porosity — can be used as Bielefeld-anchored porosity correction in our scaling law if needed

fig10_electrode_thickness_at_20pct_5um.comment: Thicknesses 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140 μm. Peak ~3.3e5 m²/m³ at composition 70/30 — INDEPENDENT OF THICKNESS.
fig10_electrode_thickness_at_20pct_5um.thin_electrode_artifact: At low AM fractions (<50%), thin electrodes (20 μm) show higher A_spec,a — finite-size effect, not real advantage
fig10_electrode_thickness_at_20pct_5um.implication: Composite coverage is intrinsic property of (composition, porosity, particle size), not of electrode thickness

bielefeld_anchors_from_fig9_porosity_swe.note: Porosity-dependent — use actual DEM porosity per case for calibration anchor, not fixed 20%

porosity_dependent_k_spread.comment: If DEM porosity varies per case, k_spread should too. k_spread(phi) = sqrt(coverage_bielefeld(phi) / coverage_plastic_dem(phi))
porosity_dependent_k_spread.caveat: Bielefeld is computational (no physical plasticity); systematic offset vs real pellets (Lee 2024) ≈ 20-25% lower for real pellets

cross_check_with_lee_2024.agreement: Lee's hand-mixed pellet 3D ≈ 0.39 vs Bielefeld computational 0.49 — same order, Bielefeld slightly higher likely because Bielefeld allows SE polyhedra overlap with AM (idealized contact), while Lee is physical ~520 MPa pressing without fully densifying contacts

comparability_with_ours.chemistry_match: CLOSE (NCM + LPS thiophosphate, like our NMC+LPSCl)
comparability_with_ours.metric_match: PARTIAL (3D volumetric — can be converted to 3D surface fraction with geometric factor)
usefulness_for_us: VALIDATES our scaling law percolation exponent ~sqrt(phi-0.2) and CN^1.5 forms
PROVIDES optimal composition anchors (80/20 wt at 5% porosity) consistent with our dataset peak
OFFERS particle-size / percolation-threshold relationship that can test CN(d) dependence
DOES NOT directly calibrate k_spread — no headline coverage value

open_questions: How to map their (AM-no-overlap + SE-polyhedra-with-overlap) to our (hard-sphere + plastic delta overlap) geometry? Their overlap-reassignment to AM already mimics plastic-like contact area expansion.
Their β=0.41 comes from electronic cluster percolation; does ionic cluster (SE-mediated) obey same exponent?
Can we regenerate their A_spec,a vs composition curves with our DEM + network solver as validation?

entries.citation: Hlushkou, Reising, Kaiser, Spannenberger, Schlabach, Kato, Roling, Tallarek. J. Power Sources 2018, 396, 363-370 — The influence of void space on ion transport in a composite cathode for all-solid-state batteries
entries.headline_finding: Tortuosity τ_cond=1.6±0.1 (EIS) ≈ τ_diff=1.74 (FIB-SEM + random walk). Bruggeman (τ_B=1.34) UNDER-predicts because 13.2% voids reshape SE topology into highly tortuous paths — not just reduce volume.
entries.pressure_match_with_ours: EXCELLENT (276 MPa electrochemistry vs our 300 MPa)
entries.chemistry_match_with_ours: PARTIAL (LCO/LPSI vs our NMC/LPSCl; both sulfide SE family)

materials.AM_particle_shape: irregular (k=3.64 vs spherical k~12 at 15% rel.stdev → highly non-spherical)

cathode_composition_initial_mixture.porosity_assumed_at_preparation: not explicitly stated — inferred as nominally zero (fully dense target)

volume_fraction_shift_from_mixture_to_re.interpretation: Both solids lose nearly identical RELATIVE fractions (~13%) to voids. LCO/SE ratio preserved (0.613 → 0.616). This is strong evidence that voids distribute UNIFORMLY in interparticle space — NOT preferentially at AM surface or SE grain boundaries.
volume_fraction_shift_from_mixture_to_re.implication_for_coverage_estimate: Supports isotropic-contact assumption: coverage_AM-SE ≈ V_SE / (V_SE + V_void) = 80.3% upper bound is likely CLOSE to true value (not just upper bound). Voids-cling-to-AM scenario rejected by data.

polydispersity_indicator_from_k_LCO.spherical_expectation: μ_chord = (2/3)·d = 3.33 μm for d=5 μm → matches measured 3.3 μm
polydispersity_indicator_from_k_LCO.interpretation: Mean chord matches ideal sphere, but k is 3x lower → LCO mean size is ~spherical-equivalent but distribution is POLYDISPERSE (wide size spread).
polydispersity_indicator_from_k_LCO.relevance_to_our_system: Our bimodal AM (P+S) is also polydisperse. Hlushkou's LCO polydispersity (k=3.64) is in the same regime, so τ comparison is not invalidated by monodisperse vs polydisperse mismatch.

reconstruction_volume_sufficiency.verdict: Just barely representative — our DEM sample (0.05×0.05×~0.02 m = 5×10^10 μm³ for pellet) is ~5 orders of magnitude larger, more statistically robust

FIB_SEM_reconstruction_details.restoration_steps: autocorrelation alignment, intensity-gradient correction, stripe removal (Xlib filter), 3D max/min smoothing, manual error correction
FIB_SEM_reconstruction_details.segmentation: MorphoLibJ morphological segmentation (gradient radius=3px, tolerance=8); minimum filter r=22px for LCO markers
FIB_SEM_reconstruction_details.finite_size_check: μ and k reach asymptotic values at ~70% of max edge (Fig S1) — volume is representative

tortuosity_Bruggeman_e_minus_0_5_at_actu.verdict: under-predicts — Bruggeman relation fails when voids redistribute SE into tortuous paths

key_results.void_free_tau_agreement: GOOD — Bruggeman valid for void-free composite with near-spherical AM

coverage_back_estimate.method: Paper does NOT report AM-SE interface coverage. Isotropic-contact approximation: coverage_AM_SE = V_SE / (V_SE + V_void) assuming random distribution
coverage_back_estimate.upgraded_confidence_due_to_uniform_loss_: MEDIUM-HIGH (was LOW) — proportional volume loss (LCO-12.9%, SE-13.4%) and preserved ratio (0.613 → 0.616) both argue AGAINST voids-cling-to-AM. Isotropic 80% is likely within ±10% of true.

SE_ionic_conductivity_comparison.ratio: Our LPSCl 2-4x faster than their LPSI
SE_ionic_conductivity_comparison.implication: Even at same τ ≈ 1.74, our σ_eff should be 2-4x higher in absolute terms. τ-based scaling law should transfer cleanly; absolute σ must use our own σ_SE baseline.

comparability_with_ours.chemistry_match: PARTIAL (LCO vs NMC — both layered oxide AM, LPSI vs LPSCl — both halide-sulfide SE)
comparability_with_ours.metric_match: DIFFERENT (they report τ, not coverage) — but coverage back-derivable
usefulness_for_us: VALIDATES that ~13% voids form even at 276 MPa pressing → our DEM should target ~13% porosity (not 20%)
SHOWS Bruggeman under-predicts τ by 20-25% in real composites → our scaling law should allow deviation from ideal ε^-0.5
CONFIRMS tortuosity ≈ 1.6-1.74 for LCO/LPSI at ~54% SE vol, useful C_blend(τ) anchor
DEMONSTRATES void elimination ↔ large σ_eff improvement (0.574 → 0.786 = 37% gain from 13% densification)

tortuosity_vs_our_scaling_law.our_C_blend_tau_term: C_blend(τ) accounts for conductor-phase tortuosity via τ^(-1)
tortuosity_vs_our_scaling_law.verdict: Hlushkou τ=1.74 falls in our typical range — consistent

SI_content_extracted.finite_size_verification: chord length μ, k reach asymptote at ~70% of max edge — reconstruction volume is representative

what_we_still_need_from_this_paper: Explicit AM-SE interface area (not reported, only chord lengths and volume fractions) — would require author contact for voxel data
Particle size DISTRIBUTION (only 'average 5 μm' given; k=3.64 implies wide spread but shape unknown)
Sintering/annealing history (only RT cold press mentioned — confirming no heat treatment would help)
Temperature of EIS measurement (frequency range 1 MHz - 0.3 mHz at RT implied)
Explicit Arrhenius data for σ_SE(T) in -120 to 160°C range (mentioned only for pure SE characterization)

entries.citation: Zhou, Park, Sun, Lalère, Adermann, Hartmann, Nazar. ACS Energy Lett. 2019, 4, 265-270 — Solvent-Engineered Design of Argyrodite Li6PS5X (X = Cl, Br, I) Solid Electrolytes with High Ionic Conductivity
entries.relevance_note: This paper is NOT about AM-SE coverage. It is about solution-engineered synthesis of argyrodite SEs. Added to DB as the σ_SE baseline reference for our scaling law (σ_grain term anchors absolute conductivity).
entries.synthesis_method: solution-engineered (Li3PS4·3THF + Li2S + LiX in THF/ethanol), sintered at 550°C in carbon-coated quartz tube under vacuum, vs ball-milled reference

pellet_prep_for_EIS.pressure_MPa_note: Pellet diameter not explicitly stated in main text; if ~13mm standard, ~150 MPa; if smaller, higher

DC_polarization_electronic_conductivity_.note: 6 orders of magnitude lower than σ_ion — confirms negligible electronic contribution to σ_total

relevance_to_our_scaling_law.which_sigma_is_our_sigma_grain: Our σ_grain corresponds to bulk SE ionic conductivity — use 2.4 mS/cm for LPSCl at RT as baseline
relevance_to_our_scaling_law.our_dataset_uses_LPSCl: Yes — this paper provides direct σ_SE anchor: σ_grain ≈ 2.4 mS/cm = 2.4e-1 S/m at 300 K
relevance_to_our_scaling_law.previous_assumed_value: Our DEM scripts implicitly assume σ_SE in ~1-3 mS/cm range — Zhou confirms 2.4 for LPSCl (mid-range)
relevance_to_our_scaling_law.halide_substitution_leverage: If future DEM cases explore mixed-halide SEs (Cl/Br), σ can go up to 3.9 mS/cm → σ_eff scales linearly with σ_SE

limitations_vs_our_work: No AM-SE coverage data — pure SE pellets only
Solution-engineered SE may have different microstructure than our ball-milled/DEM model
Pellet sintered at 550°C — our composite cathodes are RT-pressed only (different densification)
Pellet pressure in tons not MPa — cannot directly compare to our 300 MPa pellet

entries.citation: Strauss, Bartsch, de Biasi, Kim, Janek, Hartmann, Brezesinski. ACS Energy Lett. 2018, 3, 992-996 — Impact of Cathode Material Particle Size on the Capacity of Bulk-Type All-Solid-State Batteries
entries.chemistry_match_with_ours: GOOD (NCM622 matches our NCM; β-Li3PS4 vs our LPSCl — same sulfide family but different halide — LPSCl σ 2.4 mS/cm vs β-Li3PS4 0.2 mS/cm)
entries.metric_caveat_upfront: This paper reports INACTIVE CAM fraction (AM-AM electronic disconnection), NOT AM-SE ionic interface coverage. In carbon-free composites, σ_e is dominated by AM-AM percolation. In our scaling law, we need AM-SE interface (ionic coverage). These are COMPLEMENTARY metrics — electronic coverage = 1 - inactive_frac.

materials.vol_calculation_note: CAM density ~4.8, SE density ~1.84 → 70/30 wt ≈ 47.2/52.8 vol

assembly_pressures_MPa.source: SI Section: 'Electrochemical testing and cell assembly'

process.mixing: planetary ball mill 140 rpm, 30 min, Ar atmosphere, 10 × ZrO2 10 mm balls

inactive_CAM_fraction_Rietveld_exsitu_XR.measurement_method: ex-situ XRD on charged cathode (3.8 V vs In/InLi), Rietveld refinement of 003 reflection splitting into active (delithiated) + inactive (pristine SOC=0) fractions

NCM_S_inactive_pct.note: large rel error due to XRD detection limit

x_Li_at_full_charge_from_operando_XRD.note: Lower x(Li) = more delithiated; lithium content of active fraction at 3.8 V vs InLi

inactive_CAM_fraction_Rietveld_exsitu_XR.interpretation: In carbon-free composites, CAM that cannot be electronically addressed remains in pristine state (inactive). Small particles provide dense AM-AM contact network → high electronic coverage. Large particles are electronically isolated → inactive.

ncm_sieving_note_from_SI.NCM_L_origin: NCM-M sieved through 20 μm sieve, collected remainder (oversize)
ncm_sieving_note_from_SI.implication: NCM-L and NCM-M are SAME base material, different size fractions. Surface chemistry identical, only morphology differs → isolates pure size effect.

pressure_comparison_with_ours.verdict: Our 300 MPa is CLOSE to Strauss cathode-attach (375 MPa). Strauss operating pressure 55 MPa is much lower — likely causes different coverage evolution during cycling vs initial.

conductivity_partial_from_fig4.method: ion-blocking / electron-blocking electrodes on composite cathode pellet
interpretation: Ionic σ ~10^-6 S/cm is ~200x lower than bare β-Li3PS4 (2e-4 S/cm) → consistent with τ ≈ 10-20 effective reduction through composite — high tortuosity at 47 vol% NCM (near percolation threshold per Bielefeld)
Electronic σ spans 3 orders → particle-size-driven electronic percolation is the performance-limiting factor in carbon-free composites

cross_check_with_bielefeld_pc_formula.pc_at_4um: 7.83·ln(4) + 36.67  = 47.52 vol% — Strauss at 47.2 vol% → at threshold → high variability → Strauss NCM-S coverage 0.98 seems optimistic
cross_check_with_bielefeld_pc_formula.pc_at_8um: 7.83·ln(8) + 36.67  = 52.96 vol% — Strauss at 47.2 vol% → SUB-critical → low electronic coverage predicted. Strauss NCM-M 0.73 ≈ transition regime. CONSISTENT.
cross_check_with_bielefeld_pc_formula.pc_at_16um: 7.83·ln(16) + 36.67 = 58.39 vol% — Strauss at 47.2 vol% → DEEP SUB-critical → very low coverage predicted. Strauss NCM-L 0.69 suggests above-pc behavior. MODEST DISAGREEMENT (could be due to wide d90 distribution including small particles that percolate).
cross_check_with_bielefeld_pc_formula.verdict: Bielefeld prediction qualitatively matches Strauss: smaller particles → higher electronic coverage. Quantitative mismatch for NCM-L likely due to polydispersity (d90=26.1 vs d50=15.6 → wide distribution).

how_this_constrains_our_bimodal_AM.note: Our DEM radii (5 mm, 2.5 mm) are 1000x larger than Strauss NCM-S (2 μm radius). Coverage is dimensionless, so scale doesn't matter — but our bimodal (P+S) mix should behave like Strauss NCM-M polydisperse (where d50 ≈ 2x smaller in distribution).

comparability_with_ours.process_match: YES (cold-pressed ASSB pellet, RT, 7:3 wt AM:SE)
comparability_with_ours.pressure_match: UNKNOWN (not reported in main text — SI check needed)
comparability_with_ours.chemistry_match: GOOD (NCM622 + sulfide SE — closest yet in our DB)
comparability_with_ours.metric_match: COMPLEMENTARY (electronic coverage vs our target ionic coverage)
comparability_with_ours.particle_size_range: Our NCM equiv d50 range (if scaled) would bracket Strauss NCM-S through NCM-L

what_we_still_need_from_this_paper: Actual pellet pressure (MPa) — main text doesn't state; check SI
SEM cross-section images (Figure S7) of composite structure — could back-estimate AM-SE coverage visually
Sulfur EDX maps mentioned (Fig S7) — SE distribution around NCM particles
Operando XRD supplementary data (Figure S3) — SOC vs cycle progression
AM-SE ionic interface coverage — NOT provided (chemistry/pressure match but wrong metric)

takeaway_for_k_spread.conclusion: Strauss 2018 does NOT give direct AM-SE ionic coverage. It gives AM-AM electronic coverage (inactive fraction) which is a proxy for AM-AM connectivity.
takeaway_for_k_spread.implication_for_k_spread: No direct k_spread target value from this paper. However, the ionic σ ~10^-6 S/cm for composite vs 2e-4 for bare SE implies τ_effective ≈ 200 — much higher than Hlushkou 1.74. This is because Strauss is near-percolation (47 vol% AM at 47-58% pc range). Our DEM should avoid this sub-percolation regime.
takeaway_for_k_spread.revised_DB_gaps: Still need direct chemistry-exact ionic AM-SE coverage. Best candidate: Minnmann-group or Zeier-group FIB-SEM on NCM/LPSCl composite (post-2020 papers).

entries.citation: Koerver, Aygün, Leichtweiss, Dietrich, Zhang, Binder, Hartmann, Zeier, Janek. Chem. Mater. 2017, 29, 5574-5582 — Capacity Fade in Solid-State Batteries: Interphase Formation and Chemomechanical Processes in Nickel-Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes and Lithium Thiophosphate Solid Electrolytes
entries.status: PARTIAL — first 5 pages (5574-5578). Need remaining pages for XPS full results, Fig 5 spectra, cyclability detail, SI.
entries.chemistry_match_with_ours: STRONG (NCM-811 = our NMC family; β-Li3PS4 vs LPSCl — same Li3PS4-based sulfide class)
entries.pressure_match_with_ours: CLOSE (446 MPa assembly vs our 300 MPa; 64 MPa operating)
headline_findings: First-cycle CE only 70.5% in SSB vs 85.9% in LE → 15.4% irreversible loss attributed to cathode/SE interphase + chemomechanical contact loss
R_SE,Cathode grows 140 Ω (= 180 Ω·cm²) irreversibly during FIRST charge, most growth between OCV 3.2-3.4 V
SEM reveals 'severe morphological changes' in cathode microstructure after cycling — mechanical contact loss during NCM delithiation contraction
Authors directly state: 'solid cathode composites are not 100% dense, and thus, not every NCM particle is ionically and electronically well addressed'
SE has 'quite limited plasticity' → cannot flow into pores formed by AM volume change

materials.SE_composition_from_XPS: 96 at% PS4³⁻ + 4% bridging S (P2S7⁴⁻-type), may have amorphous component
materials.anode_SSB: In foil 0.125 mm, 6 mm diameter (~7.7 mAh/cm²)
materials.anode_alternative: Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) composite (tested in SI to confirm cathode-side R increase)

electrochemistry.rate_capability_test_pattern: 5 cycles each at 0.1/0.25/0.5/1 C, then ≥30 cycles at 0.1 C
electrochemistry.verdict: NCM-811/β-Li3PS4 at 47:53 vol barely functions above 0.25 C — high-C performance catastrophically poor

four_resistance_components.SOC_dependence: IRREVERSIBLE +140 Ω in 1st charge, strongest 3.2-3.4 V OCV
reversible but extreme at low Li-In alloy

CRITICAL_cathode_SE_interface_resistance.mechanism_proposed: irreversible cathode/SE interphase (CEI) from SE oxidation + chemomechanical contact loss from NCM anisotropic delithiation contraction
CRITICAL_cathode_SE_interface_resistance.direct_coverage_implication: 140 Ω increase corresponds to ~50% reduction in effective AM-SE ionic contact (if R ∝ 1/coverage). This is DYNAMIC coverage LOSS during cycling — distinct from initial pressing coverage.

chemomechanical_mechanism.NCM811_delithiation_volume_contraction_p: ~2 (c-axis expansion + a-axis shrinkage, net contraction at high SOC)
chemomechanical_mechanism.SE_plasticity: quite limited — β-Li3PS4 cannot flow into newly formed pores as AM contracts
chemomechanical_mechanism.consequence: gaps form between NCM and SE during charging → increased R_cathode/SE → capacity fade
chemomechanical_mechanism.SEM_evidence: severe morphological changes after cycling (image referenced in Fig 5, not on visible pages)
chemomechanical_mechanism.relevance_to_our_DEM: Our DEM captures INITIAL coverage (static pressing), not DYNAMIC coverage evolution during cycling. Koerver shows 140 Ω increase in first charge alone — cycling reduces effective coverage significantly even from initial state.

comparability_with_ours.pressure_match: EXCELLENT (446 MPa vs our 300 MPa, 1.49× higher)
comparability_with_ours.chemistry_match: STRONG (NCM-811 AM + sulfide β-Li3PS4; same chemistry family as our NMC/LPSCl)
comparability_with_ours.metric_match: INTERFACE RESISTANCE not coverage directly — but mappable via R ∝ 1/(coverage × σ_SE)
usefulness_for_us: Direct quantification of cathode/SE interfacial resistance = 180 Ω·cm² baseline (our scaling law σ_eff should incorporate this)
Confirms our C_blend(τ) and coverage terms need to reflect BOTH static geometric and dynamic chemomechanical effects
Sets upper bound on achievable coverage in cold-pressed pellets: even at 446 MPa, NCM particles are not fully addressed
Provides rate-capability collapse benchmark: 0.5 C → 4 mAh/g even at optimal composition → τ + coverage limits are severe

NCM811_Li3PS4_blend_NO_current.verdict: NO chemical reaction from mixing alone — confirmed by SI Fig S4 after 175 h relaxation

after_first_charge.interpretation: CEI formation via SE oxidation; P-S-(S-)n polysulfide-like species

after_50_cycles.verdict: only ~1% of SE oxidized after 50 cycles → bulk SE intact, reaction confined to thin interphase layer

XPS_fig5_interphase_quantification.quantitative_CEI_interpretation: CEI is THIN (<<1% bulk volume) but has LOW Li+ conductivity (SE decomposition products are lithium-deficient) → large interfacial R per unit area

uncycled_composite.observation: close and intimate contact between NCM edges and SE; NCM well-embedded in dense SE matrix

after_first_charge_4.3V.observation: spherical GAP forms around each NCM particle
after_first_charge_4.3V.mechanism: NCM-811 delithiation → c-axis contraction → volumetric shrinkage → pulls away from SE
after_first_charge_4.3V.SE_cannot_fill_gap_reason: β-Li3PS4 has limited plasticity at RT, cannot flow into forming voids

after_50_cycles.observation: similar morphology to 1st charge; 'negative imprint' of NCM surface on SE side
after_50_cycles.interpretation: original intimate contact evidenced by imprint — contact was LOST during first charge, not gained during pressing deficiency
after_50_cycles.ongoing_fade_attribution: CEI propagation (chemical), NOT further mechanical contact loss

SEM_fig6_DIRECT_CONTACT_LOSS_EVIDENCE.quantitative_coverage_loss_estimate: From SEM gap observation: NCM-811 ~2% c-axis contraction → gap ~50-100 nm → coverage drops from ~100% to ~60-70% in first charge (consistent with 140 Ω R growth = ~50% effective coverage reduction)

AM_contraction_vs_LCO_key_distinction.NCM_811_behavior: unit cell SHRINKS at high SOC (nickel-rich anisotropic)
AM_contraction_vs_LCO_key_distinction.implication_for_our_work: If our DEM dataset uses NCM/NMC (Ni-rich) vs LCO → our cycling-stress direction is toward CONTACT LOSS. LCO systems would have opposite trend (SE compression). Crucial for our coverage_dynamic(SOC) term if we add one.
AM_contraction_vs_LCO_key_distinction.literature_support_cited: Kondrakov 2017 (Koerver ref 41) anisotropic NCM lattice strain; Ishidzu 2016 (ref 48) lattice volume change NCM

Fig_S1_overpotential_evolution.50_cycle_avg_voltage_tracking: monotonic overpotential growth, concentrated in first few cycles

Fig_S2_capacitance_evolution_1st_2nd_cyc.C_SE_Anode_F: up to 1e-3 at end of discharge
Fig_S2_capacitance_evolution_1st_2nd_cyc.note: capacitance values STABLE across cycles — only resistances change. Confirms geometric interface area is stable; resistance change is via ρ_interphase, not area loss alone.

Fig_S3_resistance_growth_NO_current.cell_type: NCM-811/β-Li3PS4 in house-made press cell, no current applied
Fig_S3_resistance_growth_NO_current.interpretation: Resistance grows even WITHOUT electrochemistry → pure mechanical relaxation + slow interphase formation
Fig_S3_resistance_growth_NO_current.relevance_to_our_DEM: Our DEM settling phase (200k steps at 98.1 m/s² pseudo-gravity) captures this mechanical relaxation. Good — our simulation timescale matches physical relaxation timescale.

Fig_S4_XPS_blend_before_after_175h.verdict: NO chemical reaction during 175 h relaxation — Fig S3 resistance growth is purely MECHANICAL/PHYSICAL, not chemical

Fig_S5_LTO_anode_control.purpose: eliminate In anode as source of cathode-side R increase
Fig_S5_LTO_anode_control.verdict: SAME cathode-side R increase pattern observed → cathode effect is genuine, not In-anode artifact

key_insight_for_our_scaling_law.3_MECHANICAL_RELAXATION_R_growth_NO_curr: ~2× in 128 h without cycling — pure time-dependent effect
key_insight_for_our_scaling_law.takeaway_for_our_DEM: Our DEM captures STATE 1 (static pressing coverage). To predict cycled performance need:
key_insight_for_our_scaling_law.layer_b_mechanical_relaxation_factor: ~0.5x (128 h) — maps to our settling phase

entries.citation: Zhang, Weber, Weigand, Arlt, Manke, Schröder, Koerver, Leichtweiss, Hartmann, Zeier, Janek. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2017, 9, 17835-17845 — Interfacial Processes and Influence of Composite Cathode Microstructure Controlling the Performance of All-Solid-State Lithium Batteries
entries.status: COMPLETE — main text + SI fully parsed
entries.chemistry_match_with_ours: FAIR (LCO/LGPS — LCO ≠ our NCM but layered oxide; LGPS σ=5 mS/cm vs LPSCl 2.4 mS/cm)
entries.pressure_match_with_ours: CLOSE (437 MPa cathode press vs our 300 MPa)

materials.LNTO_coating_method: sol-gel (Li acetate + Nb ethoxide + Ta butoxide in ethanol, anneal 300°C overnight + 450°C 1 min)
materials.SE_synthesis: solid-state ball mill (Li2S+P2S5+GeS+S, 500 rpm 24 h, ZrO2) + seal in quartz ampule + 500°C 30 h anneal
materials.conductive_additive: NONE (carbon-free, electronic percolation via direct LCO-LCO cold-press contact)
materials.anode: In (8 mm, 0.125 mm) + Li foil (1:60 wt ratio Li:In)

process.operating_pressure: Al framework 10 N·m torque during cycling — continuous

coverage_observation_from_EDX_fig2.method: cross-sectional SEM + Co (LCO) and S (LGPS) EDX elemental maps; visual inspection of phase distribution

cell_A_50_50_wt_LCO_LGPS.observation: LCO mostly SEPARATED by SE — low LCO-LCO electronic paths but ABUNDANT LCO-SE interface

cell_B_60_40_wt.observation: balanced distribution — good LCO-SE coverage AND growing LCO-LCO contact

cell_C_70_30_wt_OPTIMAL.observation: well-balanced homogeneous distribution — cited as BEST for electrochemical performance

cell_D_80_20_wt.observation: LCO-LCO overlap abundant (good electronic) but LCO-SE contact area LIMITED — 'restricts Li+ conductivity' (authors' direct quote)

coverage_observation_from_EDX_fig2.qualitative_coverage_trend: Higher LCO fraction → more LCO-LCO (electronic) → less LCO-SE (ionic). Inflection around 70:30 wt = 49:51 vol.
coverage_observation_from_EDX_fig2.anchor_for_our_dataset: Our cases with higher NCM loading should predict reduced ionic coverage — consistent with Zhang's Cell D qualitative observation.

tomography_details.method: X-ray tomography, W anode, 80 kV, 125 μA, 1200 projections over 360°
tomography_details.reconstructs: layer thicknesses, no voids in SE separator, composite/SE interface

abstract_takeaway_for_scaling_law.key_finding: Ionic percolation → high POWER; electronic percolation → high ENERGY. Low SE fraction sufficient for high energy, higher SE fraction required for high power.
abstract_takeaway_for_scaling_law.implication_for_our_C_blend_tau_term: At low SE fraction, σ_ion dominates cell power limit → τ(composition) matters more than σ_SE itself. Our C_blend(τ) term should have stronger weight at low-SE compositions.
abstract_takeaway_for_scaling_law.implication_for_our_covered_term: Zhang explicitly states LCO-SE contact area DECREASES at high LCO loading → our coverage^(2/5) term captures exactly this dependency.

during_discharge_back_to_3.2V.R_MF_ohm: constant until 50% discharge, then mild rise

R_LF_ohm.behavior: dramatic rise — Li-In alloy kinetic hindrance at low Li

diffusion_length_fig8_CRITICAL.method: Q = Q_1C - (1/3)(d²/D_Li)·j analysis; D_Li(LCO) = 1.5e-10 m²/s assumed constant across samples
diffusion_length_fig8_CRITICAL.interpretation_as_coverage_proxy: d = average Li+ path length through AM before reaching SE. Longer d → less AM-SE contact area → worse ionic coverage. Cell D (80:20 wt) has 1.67× longer d than Cell C (70:30) despite only 27% more AM vol → STRONG coverage DECREASE at high AM loading.

relative_coverage_estimate.note: If coverage ∝ 1/d² (geometric reasoning for area available vs path squared): cov_D/cov_C ≈ (60/100)² = 0.36 → Cell D has ~36% of Cell C's AM-SE coverage
relative_coverage_estimate.alternative_estimate: If coverage ∝ 1/d: cov_D/cov_C = 0.60 → 60% of Cell C's coverage
relative_coverage_estimate.our_dataset_implication: High-AM-loading cases in our DEM should show ~0.36-0.60× coverage of optimal composition, consistent with our plastic-capped 22% at typical loading

rate_capability_fig7.interpretation: Low-SE cells (D) have high initial cap but fail at high C. High-SE cells (A, B) maintain moderate cap across all rates. Cell C (70:30) optimal balance.

retention_pct_at_100_cycles.cell_D_80_20: high initial cap then fast fade

cycle_stability_fig9.cell_D_failure_mechanism: AM particles without direct SE contact → overcharge → capacity loss. Cited with Raman evidence from Otoyama 2016 (Strauss ref 35)
cycle_stability_fig9.cell_A_low_capacity_50_pct_drop_attribut: SE excess → poor electronic percolation, unreachable LCO

SI_additional_content.Fig_S2_LNTO_coating: XRD shows LNTO coating is amorphous (no diffraction peaks added to bare LCO)

Fig_S4_S5_coating_necessity.bare_LCO_cyclability: cannot cycle reversibly (no CV peaks for LiCoO2 redox)

Fig_S9_40_60_wt_too_SE_rich.reason: electronic percolation failure — LCO particles separated by SE

SI_additional_content.Fig_S11_impedance_comparison_cells_A_to_: Same SE bulk R, varying cathode/SE and In/SE semicircles — used as composition-dependent microstructure indicator
SI_additional_content.Fig_S13_LGPS_XPS_after_100_cycles: S 2p peak broadening toward higher BE → LGPS oxidation during cycling
SI_additional_content.Fig_S14_symmetric_cells: Confirms cathode-only and anode-only resistance breakdown

cross_reference_with_our_dem_outlier_cas.hypothesis: Our thin_6, thin_9, particulate_12 outliers may correspond to Zhang's extreme compositions (near A or D) where coverage transitions happen
cross_reference_with_our_dem_outlier_cas.action_item: After full dataset re-run with plastic-corrected coverage, check if outlier cases cluster near Zhang's Cell A/D regime (low SE or low AM vol fraction)

comparability_with_ours.chemistry_match: FAIR (LCO/LGPS vs NCM/LPSCl — both layered oxide + sulfide family, different in detail)
comparability_with_ours.metric_match: QUALITATIVE ONLY (EDX observation, no τ or explicit coverage tabulated in visible pages)
usefulness_for_us: CONFIRMS our coverage term intuition (LCO-SE area decreases at high AM loading)
CONFIRMS tortuosity/τ term importance at low SE fraction
PROVIDES composition sweep (5 points) for potential quantitative validation if full paper available
DIFFERENT SE (LGPS) — coverage definition metric should be SE-agnostic for transfer
